title: TBM 410: Dancing With Problems
questions: What is actually happening today when users create initiatives, and what evidence do we have?
Which layer is the real problem at—behavior, system context, or strategic outcomes?
If we streamline initiative creation, what do we sacrifice in data quality, planning, or decision-making?
How do we help teams move between perspectives to converge on the few things that matter?
search_terms: diagnosis crux good strategy bad strategy
causal chain testing in product discovery
summary: The post argues that product teams struggle to diagnose problems because “the problem” spans multiple layers (from user behavior to organizational systems to strategic outcomes) and requires moving between many types of questions. It uses the example of initiative-creation UX to show how easy root-cause narratives can feel satisfying but fail when you test downstream effects and evidence. Intended for product leaders and discovery practitioners who need a practical way to navigate problem framing, alignment, and diagnosis over time.

title: TBM 397: The Initiative Shape Activity (And a Favor to Ask)
questions: How do we characterize the shape of an initiative so we know what kind of work and governance it needs?
What level of coordination and sustained attention will this initiative require across teams?
Where is the greatest uncertainty and how early can we de-risk through incremental progress?
Which discovery approach and decision authority model best fit this initiative’s value timing, constraints, and delay sensitivity?
summary: The post provides a practical 5-minute workshop activity for teams to independently assess an initiative’s “shape” across multiple dimensions (coordination, time horizon, value timing, uncertainty, constraints, delay sensitivity, discovery approach, and decision authority). It’s aimed at leaders and product/engineering teams who want a more actionable way to surface ambiguity, drive alignment, and decide what to clarify or discover next. The author also invites participants to take the survey and feed back improvements for later integration into a prioritization tool.

title: TBM 396: So You Want To Define “The Problem”?
questions: What problem does the customer describe in their own words, and what narrative is organizing their understanding?
How do other actors interpret the problem, and how might our presence distort what we observe?
What frictions, incentives, norms, habits, or power dynamics are reinforcing current behaviors?
Given what we’ve learned and what’s been tried before, what can we realistically influence today with our product or expertise?
search_terms: COM-B opportunity capability motivation product interventions
garbage can theory solution holders searching for problems
summary: This post challenges the common product mantra to “define the problem” by arguing that problem framing is inherently multi-perspective, contested, and shaped by context. It offers a layered approach for product builders and interveners—starting with customer narratives, expanding to ecosystem interpretations and system forces, integrating history of prior attempts, and ending with hypothesis-driven, feasible intervention design. The guidance is aimed at teams doing discovery and deciding what to build when the “real problem” is unclear.

title: TBM 214: From Assumption to Next Step
questions: How do we move from assumption mapping to a clear next action without getting stuck in analysis paralysis?
What language should we use to express different levels of confidence in an assumption?
How can we incorporate evidence and observations when stating assumptions?
What prompt structure helps teams propose next steps tied to specific assumptions?
summary: The post offers a practical communication approach for product leaders to turn assumption mapping into decisions and experiments. It focuses on how to phrase assumptions with calibrated confidence, include evidence signals, and attach a concrete next step to prevent analysis paralysis. It’s aimed at teams and leaders who want to make uncertainty visible without stalling on open-ended discussion.

title: TBM 212: A Problem vs. The Problem
questions: When someone asks for a clear definition of the problem, which level of causality are they actually asking for?
How do we distinguish an adverse effect from contributing factors and root causes in everyday work?
Why do problem discussions become political or identity-driven instead of solution-oriented?
What practices help teams communicate about problems more precisely?
summary: The post argues that teams often confuse different levels of causality when discussing “problems,” leading to circular conversations. It provides a practical framing of problems as adverse effects, contributing/immediate causes, and underlying/systemic factors, and explains how problem definitions become political negotiations. It’s aimed at practitioners in RCA, safety, incident analysis, and human factors who want clearer, more actionable problem communication.

title: TBM 31/52: Those Pesky Question-Askers
questions: How do you handle an experienced person who asks lots of questions in a strategy meeting?
When does “tabling” questions help, and when does it squelch the best ideas?
Why do less experienced team members ask fewer questions, and how does that affect outcomes?
How can leaders leverage experienced contributors’ curiosity without letting meetings stall?
search_terms: experienced people ask lots of questions meeting
summary: The post argues that experienced contributors ask many questions because they’re actively gathering information to make better decisions, and that leaders should not silence them when their questions feel distracting. It highlights how repeatedly tabling questions can trigger frustration and detachment, reducing the quality of ideas the team generates. Aimed at leaders facilitating strategy/product discussions with experienced team members who need to be leveraged rather than shut down.

title: TBM 31/52: Bet Up Activity
questions: How can we make our implicit strategy explicit using current work-in-progress?
What workshop activity can help teams reverse engineer a driver tree from real efforts?
Do teams’ current initiatives connect to meaningful outcomes we can measure?
Where are we missing measurement focus or actionable levers?
search_terms: strategy flip from work in progress
North Star strategy down vs bet up
summary: This post presents a workshop facilitation activity (“Bet Up”) for product and strategy teams to reverse engineer a driver tree from current work-in-progress. It is designed for groups that want to surface implicit strategy, align efforts to meaningful outcomes, and scan for measurement focus and missing levers. The author emphasizes that the method scales to many teams and tends to converge on shared drivers.

title: TBM 17/52: The Problem With Problems
questions: Is this problem worth solving now, or is there a higher-leverage opportunity?
Is the problem actually solvable under our constraints, or will we only learn by trying?
How do we prevent problem definition from becoming a political negotiation that biases decisions?
Should we wait to define the problem perfectly or take action to learn about it?
search_terms: decision making under uncertainty safe to not know
problem wrangling exploration definition appraise solve
summary: This post argues that the common separation between problem definition (PM) and problem solving (team) is often misleading because problems are nested within higher-level problems and are shaped by context. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who need practical guidance for evaluating whether a problem is worth solving and solvable, while avoiding political/binary dynamics that cause premature certainty. The post recommends opportunity framing, iterative problem wrangling, and embracing messiness and assumptions.

title: TBM 4/52: An Actionable Post With Examples (Tree/Loop Prompts)
questions: How can I make tree/loop diagrams in a more actionable, repeatable way?
What prompts help teams turn guesses into testable causal hypotheses?
How do I break down a problem into contributing factors and sub-assumptions?
How can I use reverse prompts to explore alternative directions and constraints?
summary: The post provides a set of practical, copyable prompts for building tree and loop diagrams (including causal relationship diagrams, assumption maps, and inputs-to-outputs models). It’s aimed at product managers and workshop facilitators who want a less “thinky” entry point to diagramming so teams can surface assumptions, constraints, and causal expectations. It also shows how to combine and nest prompts and how reversing statements can help explore alternative directions.

title: TBM 20/52: A Product Super-Skill (Balancing Divergence and Convergence)
questions: Is my organization converging too early or taking too long to converge—and how can both happen at once?
How do we avoid a 'there-but-not-there' strategy that doesn’t guide decisions?
What practices help manage uncertainty without rushing to plans or getting stuck in endless exploration?
How should we time decisions and agreements to improve execution?
summary: The post explores how product teams and organizations get stuck in a harmful loop between premature convergence on plans and delayed convergence on understanding. It’s aimed at product leaders and practitioners who need a practical way to balance divergence and convergence, rebuild trust, and manage uncertainty through learning-oriented strategy rather than faux certainty. It also emphasizes improving the timing and flow of decisions so agreement and execution happen at the right moments.

title: TBM 13/52: Asking Better Questions (Part 2)
questions: How can we elicit better questions to guide analytics and qualitative research?
What framework should we use to connect strategy, opportunities, and interventions to the questions we ask?
How do we map question types to divergence vs convergence so we don’t jump to premature answers?
Which questions are most valuable to reduce uncertainty, and what measurement approach is minimally viable?
summary: The post presents a reusable board (inspired by a triple diamond) for eliciting higher-quality questions that connect strategy, opportunities, and interventions to decisions, assumptions, and impact. It’s aimed at product teams and analysts who need better question framing for both quantitative analytics and qualitative research, using divergence/convergence and practical question tools like FINER and PICOT.

title: TBM 7/52: Risk Checklist Activity
questions: How can our team talk about risk and uncertainty in a way that leads to better planning?
What initiative risks are we missing when our risk section is only a few generic bullets?
How do we ensure our risk checklist is specific, actionable, and not just theater?
How can we validate and improve a risk checklist using past projects?
search_terms: pressure test risk questions with historical efforts
summary: The post proposes a practical, team-run activity for creating and using a checklist of initiative risk questions to improve how teams discuss uncertainty. It is aimed at product and project teams that currently treat risk as a one-time PRD section or focus narrowly on schedule risks. The guidance emphasizes specificity, cross-functional input, historical validation, continuous revision, and avoiding performative review dynamics.

title: TBM 38/53: Assumptions: Value & Certainty
questions: Which assumptions in our product are the riskiest, and is the risk driven by uncertainty or by high value?
Where would decreasing uncertainty produce the most value for our team?
How can we protect high-value, highly certain information while still exploring diverse perspectives?
How do we leverage commoditized knowledge to generate new, less certain but potentially valuable ideas?
summary: The post offers a framework for thinking about product assumptions as a team belief system, emphasizing coherence rather than uniform agreement. It explains how startups’ assumptions often evolve from uncertain bets to higher certainty as competitors catch up, making information more commoditized. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams who want practical ways to brainstorm, categorize, and manage assumptions by uncertainty and potential value.

title: TBM 35/53: Basic Prioritization Questions (and When to Converge on a Solution)
questions: How much solutioning should we do before starting an effort?
Which uncertainties matter most right now, and how confident are we about each?
When should we experiment to de-risk instead of settling on a solution?
What happens to risk if we converge on a solution early?
search_terms: when to converge on a solution
summary: This post argues that product teams should delay solutioning until they’ve clarified key uncertainties, especially opportunity value and opportunity size. It offers a practical set of prioritization questions and confidence-based reasoning to decide when to experiment versus when to converge on a solution. It’s aimed at product managers, designers, and engineers who want better alignment and lower risk in early product development.

title: TBM 24/53: Mapping Beliefs (and Agreeing to Disagree)
questions: Which team beliefs are worth converging on, and which convergence is actually harmful?
When should we agree, and when should we agree to disagree?
How do we surface and map beliefs when people struggle to articulate them?
How can we tell when agreement has become stale and needs to be re-examined?
summary: The post introduces a simple belief-convergence matrix for teams to place shared beliefs based on how much they converge and whether that convergence helps or harms outcomes. It’s aimed at leaders and facilitators who want better conversations about product strategy, executive disagreements, and when to shift from alignment to agreeing to disagree. It also highlights how agreement can become stale and how re-challenging it can create productive uncertainty before moving back toward convergence.
